resume:
Product Management Intern Candidate

Third-year Information Systems student seeking a summer PM internship. Comfortable with SQL, basic A/B test analysis, and writing PRDs.

Experience
- Campus Dining App (student project, team of 4) — acted as product lead. Shipped a mobile pre-order feature over one semester.
- Course project: redesigned a library room-booking flow; ran 6 usability sessions.

Skills
- SQL, Figma, basic Python (pandas), Amplitude.
- Wrote PRDs and a simple RICE-style prioritization sheet.

Education
- B.S. Information Systems, expected next year. GPA 3.7.

Note: no professional PM experience yet; all work is coursework or student-org projects.
jd: Our consumer fintech app is hiring a Summer Product Management Intern to support the Growth pod. You will partner with a senior PM on the new-user onboarding funnel, help write tickets and PRDs, pull funnel data in Amplitude/SQL, and help design and read out at least one A/B test during the internship. We want someone who can speak precisely about what they personally did on a project, how they measured it, and what the result was — clarity and intellectual honesty matter more than scope. Strong candidates can describe a specific metric they moved (or tried to) and explain what they would do differently. This is a 12-week paid internship.

history.q: Tell me about the campus dining pre-order feature — what was your role and what did you actually ship?
How did you decide pre-order was worth building over other ideas the team had?
Did you measure whether anyone used it? What happened?

candidate_last_answer: Yes, but I'll be honest about the limits. We shipped to about 320 students in a two-week pilot. I instrumented three Amplitude events — order_started, order_submitted, pickup_confirmed. In week one, 47 students started an order but only 19 submitted, so a 40% completion rate. I dug into the drop-off and found the time-slot picker was the main blocker: it defaulted to a slot that was often sold out. I changed the default to the next available slot, and in week two submissions went from 19 to 41. I can't claim long-term retention — the pilot ended with the semester, so I don't have data past two weeks.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I changed the default to the next available slot, and in week two submissions went from 19 to 41

expected-yield: whether the candidate can reason about confounds — that a default change coincided with week-two familiarity and a different cohort — and what they would have instrumented to isolate the default change as the true cause
